title: ExtendingCDMGsWith
tex_block_html:
Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $W \ins J \cup V$ a subset of nodes. The extended CDMG of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W \ins J \cup V$ and corresponding intervention nodes

$I_W = \{I_w \,|\, w \in W\}$ with $I_j:=j$ for $j \in J \cap W$, is the CDMG: \[ G_{\doit(I_W)}:=(J_{\doit(I_W)},V_{\doit(I_W)},E_{\doit(I_W)},L_{\doit(I_W)}),\]

where:

  1. $J_{\doit(I_W)}:= J\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w\,|\, w \in W \sm J \rC$,
  2. $V_{\doit(I_W)}:= V$,
  3. $E_{\doit(I_W)}:= E\, \dot{\cup} \, \lC I_w \tuh w \,|\, w \in W\sm J \rC$,
  4. $L_{\doit(I_W)}:= L$,

where we just add nodes $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$ and edges $I_w \tuh w$ for $ w \in W \sm J$.

tex_statement.html:
Let $G = (J, V, E, L)$ be a CDMG (def def-cdmg); throughout we use the notation of def not-cdmg and recall the typing and axioms of def def-cdmg, namely $J \cap V = \emptyset$, $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$, $L \ins V \times V$, $L$ irreflexive ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Rightarrow v_1 \neq v_2$), and $L$ symmetric ($(v_1, v_2) \in L \Leftrightarrow (v_2, v_1) \in L$). Let $W \ins J \cup V$ be a subset of nodes; we do not require $W \cap J = \emptyset$, i.e.\ $W$ is permitted to overlap the input-node set $J$.

The extended CDMG of $G$ w.r.t.\ nodes $W$ and corresponding intervention nodes $I_W := \lC I_w \st w \in W \rC$ is the CDMG \[ G_{\doit(I_W)} \;:=\; \lp J_{\doit(I_W)},\, V_{\doit(I_W)},\, E_{\doit(I_W)},\, L_{\doit(I_W)} \rp, \] constructed as follows.

Fresh intervention nodes. For each $w \in W \sm J$ we introduce a single fresh intervention symbol $I_w$: a brand-new element of a formally distinct kind from every element of $J \cup V$ and from every other $I_{w'}$ with $w' \in W \sm J$, $w' \neq w$. In a formal encoding, the family \[ \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \] is realised as a tagged copy of $W \sm J$ (e.g.\ via a $\Sigma$-type or tagged sum); concretely, the downstream Lean rendering uses an inductive tagged-sum carrier with one constructor that lifts every original node of $J \cup V$ and a separate constructor that produces the new symbol $I_w$ for each $w \in W \sm J$. Under this convention, the disjointness assertions \[ \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap (J \cup V) \;=\; \emptyset, \qquad \text{and} \qquad I_{w_1} \neq I_{w_2} \text{ for distinct } w_1, w_2 \in W \sm J, \] hold automatically by typing rather than being asserted as a side condition; in particular each fresh symbol $I_w$ is type-disjoint from $J$, from $V$, and from every other fresh $I_{w'}$.

Notational shorthand $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$. For $j \in J \cap W$ we put $I_j := j$ purely as a notational shorthand: the symbol $I_j$ is a label for the pre-existing input node $j \in J$ itself, and no new node is introduced on the $J \cap W$ branch. In particular, the pre-existing $j \in J$ retains in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ all edges that $G$ already assigned to it, with no fresh symbol added next to it and no extra edge added pointing at it. This convention is needed solely so that $I_W = \lC I_w \st w \in W \rC$ is well-defined as a set indexed by the entirety of $W$: without it, the symbol $I_w$ would have no referent on the $J \cap W$ branch. With the convention, $I_W$ decomposes as \[ I_W \;=\; (J \cap W) \;\cup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC, \] where the first component contains the (notational) $I_j = j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ and the second component contains the genuinely fresh symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W \sm J$, the latter type-disjoint from the former by the freshness clause.

With these conventions in place, the four components of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$ are defined as follows.

  1. Input nodes: \[ J_{\doit(I_W)} \;:=\; J \;\cup\; \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC. \] The LN's $\dot\cup$ between $J$ and $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ is automatically disjoint: by the freshness clause, every $I_w$ with $w \in W \sm J$ is type-disjoint from every element of $J$, so $J \cap \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC = \emptyset$ holds without a separate side condition.
  2. Output nodes: \[ V_{\doit(I_W)} \;:=\; V. \]
  3. Directed edges: \[ E_{\doit(I_W)} \;:=\; E \;\cup\; \lC (I_w,\, w) \st w \in W \sm J \rC. \] The LN's $\dot\cup$ between $E$ and $\lC (I_w, w) \st w \in W \sm J \rC$ is automatically disjoint: every pair $(I_w, w)$ in the second set-builder has its source $I_w$ in $\lC I_{w'} \st w' \in W \sm J \rC$, which by the freshness clause is type-disjoint from $J \cup V$; but every edge in $E$ has its source in $J \cup V$ (by $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$), so no edge of $E$ can coincide with any $(I_w, w)$. Equivalently, rendered through def not-cdmg, item 2: every directed edge $v_1 \tuh v_2$ already in $E$ is retained, and additionally a single new directed edge $I_w \tuh w$ is included for every $w \in W \sm J$ (and none for $w \in J \cap W$).
  4. Bidirected edges: \[ L_{\doit(I_W)} \;:=\; L. \]

CDMG axioms for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The CDMG axioms (def def-cdmg) hold for $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, with the new intervention nodes landing in $J_{\doit(I_W)}$ and the bidirected side and output side untouched:

  • Input–output disjointness, $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cap V_{\doit(I_W)} = \emptyset$: $J \cap V = \emptyset$ by hypothesis on $G$, and $\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap V = \emptyset$ by the freshness clause (each $I_w$ is type-disjoint from $V$); since $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$ and $J_{\doit(I_W)} = J \cup \lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC$, distribution of $\cap$ over $\cup$ gives $J_{\doit(I_W)} \cap V_{\doit(I_W)} = (J \cap V) \cup (\lC I_w \st w \in W \sm J \rC \cap V) = \emptyset$.
  • Directed-edge typing, $E_{\doit(I_W)} \ins (J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}) \times V_{\doit(I_W)}$: for every $(v_1, v_2) \in E$ we have $v_1 \in J \cup V \ins J_{\doit(I_W)} \cup V_{\doit(I_W)}$ and $v_2 \in V = V_{\doit(I_W)}$, by the hypothesis $E \ins (J \cup V) \times V$ on $G$; for every new edge $(I_w, w)$ with $w \in W \sm J$, the source $I_w$ lies in $\lC I_{w'} \st w' \in W \sm J \rC \ins J_{\doit(I_W)}$, and the target $w$ lies in $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$ (the latter because $w \in W \sm J$ together with $W \ins J \cup V$ forces $w \in V$).
  • Bidirected-edge typing, $L_{\doit(I_W)} \ins V_{\doit(I_W)} \times V_{\doit(I_W)}$: since $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$ and $V_{\doit(I_W)} = V$, this is exactly the hypothesis $L \ins V \times V$ on $G$, transported unchanged.
  • Irreflexivity of $L_{\doit(I_W)}$: since $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$, this is exactly irreflexivity of $L$, transported unchanged.
  • Symmetry of $L_{\doit(I_W)}$: since $L_{\doit(I_W)} = L$, this is exactly symmetry of $L$, transported unchanged.

In particular, none of the three $L$-axioms requires re-checking: $L$ is unchanged and $V$ is unchanged, so the three bidirected-side axioms transport directly from $G$. Only the directed-edge typing and the input–output disjointness involve genuinely new content, and both reduce to the freshness clause above.

Informally: for every node $w \in W \sm J$ we add a single fresh intervention node $I_w$ to the input side, together with a single new directed edge $I_w \tuh w$ pointing at $w$ (which by $W \ins J \cup V$ and $w \notin J$ must lie in $V$); for every node $w \in J \cap W$ we add nothing, since the identification $I_w := w$ is purely notational and the pre-existing input node $w$ plays the role of its own intervention node. The output-node set $V$ and the bidirected-edge set $L$ are not touched. Each fresh intervention node $I_w$ (for $w \in W \sm J$) has exactly one outgoing directed edge in $G_{\doit(I_W)}$, namely $I_w \tuh w$, and is the target of no edge of $G_{\doit(I_W)}$: it is the target of no pre-existing edge in $E$ (since $I_w \notin J \cup V$, while every edge in $E$ has its target in $V$ and so cannot point at $I_w$), and it is the target of no new edge in $\lC (I_{w'}, w') \st w' \in W \sm J \rC$ (since each such edge has its target $w' \in V$, type-disjoint from $I_w$).

lean_blocks.explanation: This is a `variable` declaration: Lean auto-binds `Node : Type*` and the instance `[DecidableEq Node]` as implicit arguments to every subsequent declaration in the surrounding `CDMG` namespace, sparing us from rewriting the same parameters on `extendingCDMGsWith`. The `Type*` notation makes `Node` universe-polymorphic, so callers can instantiate it at any universe level. The `DecidableEq Node` assumption is load-bearing for the extension construction in particular: the body uses `Finset.image` (to lift `G.J`, `G.V`, `G.E`, `G.L` through the tagged-sum constructors and to build the fresh-symbol images on `W \ G.J`), `Finset.union` (to assemble the new `J` and `E` fields from two image pieces), and `Finset.sdiff` (for `W \ G.J`) — all of which need decidable equality on the element type to compute the resulting `Finset`. The lifted instance `DecidableEq (IntExtNode Node)` that `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)` requires is synthesised automatically by the next block's `deriving DecidableEq` clause from this `DecidableEq Node`. Stronger structure on `Node` (e.g.\ `Fintype`, `LinearOrder`) is not required here and is deferred to downstream consumers that actually use it. Same convention as the rest of `Chapter3_GraphTheory`'s files.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Auto-binds `Node` + `DecidableEq Node` as implicits; decidability drives `Finset.image`/`union`/`sdiff` below.
variable {Node : Type*} [DecidableEq Node]

lean_blocks.code:
inductive IntExtNode (Node : Type*) where
  | unsplit (v : Node) : IntExtNode Node
  | intCopy (w : Node) : IntExtNode Node
  deriving DecidableEq
lean_blocks.explanation: This block introduces `IntExtNode Node`, the carrier type of the extended CDMG $G_{\doit(I_W)}$. The LN demands that each fresh intervention symbol $I_w$ (for $w \in W \setminus J$) be type-disjoint from every element of $J \cup V$ and from every other $I_{w'}$. Rather than carry these as `Finset.Disjoint` side conditions, the construction realises them at the type level: `IntExtNode` is a fresh `inductive` with two named constructors. The `unsplit v` constructor lifts an arbitrary original node $v \in J \cup V$ into the extended carrier, and `intCopy w` produces the fresh intervention symbol $I_w$. Constructor disjointness (`unsplit v ≠ intCopy w`) and constructor injectivity (`intCopy w₁ = intCopy w₂ → w₁ = w₂`) hold for free, so the LN's freshness claims become typing facts. The constructor name `unsplit` is borrowed across section 3.2 so cross-row pattern matches read identically; `intCopy` self-documents as `intervention copy of w`. The `deriving DecidableEq` clause asks Lean to synthesise the `DecidableEq (IntExtNode Node)` instance from the ambient `DecidableEq Node`, satisfying what `CDMG (IntExtNode Node)` requires. This helper sits between the `variable` block and the main `def`; the main `def`'s field assignments invoke both constructors via dot-notation.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- Tagged-sum carrier for the extended CDMG; constructor disjointness encodes LN freshness at the type level.
inductive IntExtNode (Node : Type*) where
  -- Lifts every original `v ∈ J ∪ V` into the extended carrier; name shared across section 3.2.
  | unsplit (v : Node) : IntExtNode Node
  -- Produces the fresh intervention symbol `I_w` for each `w ∈ W \ J`; type-disjoint from `unsplit`.
  | intCopy (w : Node) : IntExtNode Node
  -- Lifts `DecidableEq Node` to `DecidableEq (IntExtNode Node)`, which the result `CDMG` requires.
  deriving DecidableEq

lean_blocks.code:
def extendingCDMGsWith (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : CDMG (IntExtNode Node) where
  J := G.J.image IntExtNode.unsplit ∪ (W \ G.J).image IntExtNode.intCopy
  V := G.V.image IntExtNode.unsplit
  hJV_disj := extendingCDMGsWith_hJV_disj G W
  E := G.E.image (fun e => (IntExtNode.unsplit e.1, IntExtNode.unsplit e.2))
        ∪ (W \ G.J).image (fun w => (IntExtNode.intCopy w, IntExtNode.unsplit w))
  hE_subset := extendingCDMGsWith_hE_subset G W hW
  L := G.L.image (Sym2.map IntExtNode.unsplit)
  hL_subset := extendingCDMGsWith_hL_subset G
  hL_irrefl := extendingCDMGsWith_hL_irrefl G
lean_blocks.explanation: This block constructs $G \cup \{W\}^{\text{int}}$, the CDMG extended with $W$'s nodes as fresh intervention copies. The carrier type CHANGES from `Node` to `IntExtNode Node` — the inductive sum of `unsplit` (lifts original nodes) and `intCopy` (adds intervention copies for $W \setminus J$, i.e. the elements of $W$ that weren't already input nodes). Input set: relabelled $J$ plus the new `intCopy` images of $W \setminus J$. Output set: relabelled $V$ unchanged. Directed-edge set: original edges relabelled via `unsplit`, plus glue edges `(IntExtNode.intCopy w, IntExtNode.unsplit w)` connecting each intervention copy to its original. Bidirected-edge set: original pairs lifted via `Sym2.map IntExtNode.unsplit`. The four obligations are discharged by lifted `private lemma`s above the def. `hE_subset` consumes `hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` to argue that every $w \in W \setminus J$ has a typed endpoint in the output set; the other three obligations don't need `hW`.
lean_blocks.code_annotated:
-- ref: def_3_13 (main) — extend `G` with `W`'s nodes as `IntExtNode.intCopy` copies; obligations lifted.
def extendingCDMGsWith (G : CDMG Node) (W : Finset Node)
    -- `hW` records the LN's `W ⊆ J ∪ V`; consumed by `hE_subset` for the glue-edge typing.
    (hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V) : CDMG (IntExtNode Node) where
  -- Input set: relabelled `J` plus the `intCopy` images of `W \ J` (the nodes being newly intervened on).
  J := G.J.image IntExtNode.unsplit ∪ (W \ G.J).image IntExtNode.intCopy
  -- Output set: relabelled `V` unchanged.
  V := G.V.image IntExtNode.unsplit
  hJV_disj := extendingCDMGsWith_hJV_disj G W
  -- Edges: original edges relabelled via `unsplit`, plus glue edges from each `intCopy w` to `unsplit w`.
  E := G.E.image (fun e => (IntExtNode.unsplit e.1, IntExtNode.unsplit e.2))
        ∪ (W \ G.J).image (fun w => (IntExtNode.intCopy w, IntExtNode.unsplit w))
  hE_subset := extendingCDMGsWith_hE_subset G W hW
  -- Bidirected `Sym2` pairs lifted into the `unsplit`-side via `Sym2.map`.
  L := G.L.image (Sym2.map IntExtNode.unsplit)
  hL_subset := extendingCDMGsWith_hL_subset G
  hL_irrefl := extendingCDMGsWith_hL_irrefl G

addition_to_the_LN: [I_W_mixes_fresh_nodes_and_existing_context_nodes] Intervention nodes are by default fresh nodes disjoint from $J \cup V$, not present in $G$ prior to the intervention. For $w \in W \sm J$, $I_w$ is such a new symbol, added to the junction set with a single outgoing edge $I_w \tuh w$. For $w \in J \cap W$, the identification $I_w := w$ introduces no new node and serves only as a notational convenience so that $I_W$ is well-defined as a set; here the pre-existing context node $w \in J$ retains whatever edges $G$ already assigned to it.
design_choices:
**`def`, not `structure` / `inductive` / `class`; result carrier is `IntExtNode Node`, not `Node`.** Extending with intervention nodes is a function `CDMG Node → Finset Node → … → CDMG (IntExtNode Node)`, not new data — mirrors the sibling §3.2 operator pattern (`def_3_10`–`def_3_12`). The carrier swap is the load-bearing departure from `def_3_10`'s hard intervention: fresh symbols $I_w$ for $w \in W \setminus J$ must be type-disjoint from `Node` and from each other, and the `[I_W_mixes_fresh_nodes_and_existing_context_nodes]` clause fixes this at the type level via the two-constructor `inductive IntExtNode` (previous helper). The LN freshness assertions $\{I_w\} \cap (J \cup V) = \emptyset$ and $I_{w_1} \neq I_{w_2}$ become typing facts via constructor disjointness and injectivity, not `Disjoint` proof obligations.

**`hW : W ⊆ G.J ∪ G.V` is explicit, consumed only by `hE_subset`.** Subset facts are not typeclass-resolvable, so an implicit/instance binder would force callers to discharge membership manually. Of the four proof obligations, only `hE_subset` uses `hW` — to derive $w \in G.V$ from $w \in W \setminus G.J$ for the target of each new edge `(.intCopy w, .unsplit w)`.

**`Sym2.map IntExtNode.unsplit` for `L'`, not `Sym2.lift`, not `Sym2.mk` destructuring.** Under `cdmg_typed_edges`, item iv becomes `G.L.image (Sym2.map IntExtNode.unsplit) : Finset (Sym2 (IntExtNode Node))`. `Sym2.lift` would force a function-of-pairs plus a trivial swap-symmetry proof at every L-site; representative destructuring would force a canonical ordered-pair choice the `Sym2` quotient has no canonical choice for. `Sym2.map` is Mathlib's canonical quotient-functorial lift — `Sym2.map f s(a, b) = s(f a, f b)` definitionally — paired with the `Sym2.mem_map` / `Sym2.isDiag_map` API the proof obligations need. Items i–iii use plain `Finset.image` (J/V/E carry their ordinary `Finset` typing).

**Intervention edges are directed in `E'`, never bidirected in `L'`; the shorthand $I_j := j$ for $j \in J \cap W$ is captured implicitly by the carrier shape.** LN clause iii adds `I_w → w` — a typed directed arrow. A `Sym2`-typed alternative would lose direction and falsely admit a reversed edge $w \to I_w$ the LN does not introduce; downstream walk-reversal (`claim_3_22`) classifies the channel by carrier type and relies on this typing. Separately, every `intCopy`-ranging clause iterates over $W \setminus G.J$, never over $W$ — no `IntExtNode.intCopy j` is created for $j \in J \cap W$, and the pre-existing `IntExtNode.unsplit j` is the sole inhabitant of the $I_W$-slot on that branch.

**Four `private lemma` discharges, matching `CDMG`'s four propositional fields.** Each CDMG axiom (`hJV_disj`, `hE_subset`, `hL_subset`, `hL_irrefl`) is a named helper above the `def`; the body is then pure data — four field assignments aligned with LN items i–iv plus four helper references, no tactics. There is no `_hL_symm` companion: on `L : Finset (Sym2 Node)` swap-symmetry is definitional, so `CDMG` carries no `hL_symm` field. `hL_subset` / `hL_irrefl` discharge via `Sym2.mem_map` / `Sym2.isDiag_map` with constructor injectivity of `.unsplit`; `hJV_disj` / `hE_subset` are direct `Finset.image`/`Finset.mem_image` discharges. Argument order `(G) (W) (hW)` enables dot-notation `G.extendingCDMGsWith W hW`; named-field `where` syntax keeps each assignment adjacent to its LN item.
